problem_statement: AI coding agents increasingly author pull requests, but whether—and in what task contexts—they introduce breaking changes (backward-compatibility violations) versus human developers is underexplored, making the reliability of AI-generated PRs hard to assess for review.
magnitude_evidence: OpenAI Codex created more than 400,000 PRs in open-source GitHub repositories within the first two months of its release [19]; study analyzed 7,191 agent-generated vs 1,402 human-authored PRs from AIDev
insufficiency_evidence: Breaking changes occur across all high-confidence levels (8–10), ranging from 3.16% to 3.96%, indicating that AI agents' confidence scores alone do not guarantee safe code; 'confidence scores alone are insufficient for prioritizing review or deployment'
solution_mechanisms: AST-based breaking-change detector from Git diffs
